original_abstract:
Digital twin (DT) technology has garnered significant attention across science and engineering due to its potential to revolutionize asset management and operational efficiency. However, the exploration of DT remains fragmented, with manufacturing, aerospace, and oil and gas industries leading the way while civil engineering lags behind. Currently, the civil engineering sector often relies on adapted techniques from these external fields, resulting in a narrow application of DT primarily focused on the operations and maintenance phase. In contrast, Building Information Modeling (BIM) dominates the planning and design stages, yet the highly transient and complex nature of the construction phase continues to hinder seamless DT adoption.

In this article, we propose a comprehensive, phase-based development framework for DT within the architecture, engineering, and construction (AEC) industry. We begin by clarifying the conceptual definitions of DT as both a product and a service, establishing a standardized five-level maturity scale to categorize its capabilities. Furthermore, we conduct a systematic literature review of conventional and emerging techniques utilized at each stage of a civil engineering project—from design through to demolition. In doing so, we identify key enabling technologies, such as computer vision for enhanced environmental sensing and the Internet of Things (IoT) for robust data integration. Ultimately, this work repositions the digital twin as a vital, holistic tool for the entire project life cycle, urging researchers and practitioners to move beyond segmented applications toward an integrated, life-cycle-oriented approach for civil engineering systems.
humanized_abstract:
The use of Digital twin (DT) technology has surged across the realms of science and engineering with promises of greatly improving the management and operation of existing assets. Despite the promising developments, the exploration of DT is scattered and has been predominantly focused on a handful of industries such as manufacturing, aerospace and oil and gas. Meanwhile, civil engineering has not had a deep-seated exploration into the use of DT technology. In fact, most existing researches relating to civil engineering are based on adopted technologies from external fields and as such the applications of DT in civil engineering is highly limited and mostly constrained to the operation and maintenance stage. Similarly, while Building Information Modeling (BIM) is being increasingly adopted in the design stage of construction, the DT technology is proven to be less effective in modelling the highly dynamic and intricate nature of the construction process.

This article puts forward a phase-based development guideline for Digital Twin (DT) in the Architecture, Engineering and Construction (AEC) industry. After introducing the DT as a product and service with defined conceptual features at different maturity levels in a five-level DT capability maturity scale, the authors take a snapshot of conventional and new generation techniques used in different phases of a civil engineering project from design to demolition and behind the key-enabling technologies such as computer vision for environmental sensing and Internet of Things (IoTs) for data harvesting. In conclusion, the authors highlight the potential of digital twin to evolve as an all-encompassing and comprehensive tool that supports all phases of a civil engineering project and advocate for holistic and life cycle-oriented approach to DT in civil engineering that moves away from the current fragmented and isolated uses of DT and enhances the implementation of the concept.
